paper_title: Fresh Tomatoes: Ideas to Build a Productive Eco-System
research_answer: The European and Eurasian fresh tomato export market is highly concentrated: the Netherlands and Spain alone hold 55% of the regional export share, and the top 10 exporters account for virtually 100% of all exports — with only two of them classifiable as developing countries. Albania's share is close to 0%. Despite doubling its tomato exports between 2011 and 2013, Albania's participation remains negligible in regional terms. The dominance of Netherlands and Spain also reflects a quality and technology gap: these countries achieve yields roughly three times higher than Albania's (~330,000 Hg/Ha), command prices two to three times higher per kilogram, and have sophisticated supply chains with established long-term client relationships. Albania, by contrast, sells almost entirely to lower-value neighboring markets on a spot-transaction basis.
supporting_quotes: Netherlands and Spain take 55% of the share and only two of the top 10 exporters (which represent virtually 100% of the exports) can be considered developing countries. Second, Albania's share is close to 0%
the share of Albania in the European trade is almost negligible
exports showed an important growth (in tomatoes, for example exports doubled from 2013 to 2011!)
Albanian yield was virtually identical to the yield of Turkish producers (approximately 330,000 Hg/Ha)
the yield in Albania for fresh tomatoes is about a third as big as the yield in a competitive market like France
